attributes.stem:
A 23-year-old man is admitted to the rehabilitation ward following eight weeks in the acute hospital after sustaining a severe traumatic brain injury in a motor vehicle accident, with a severe haemorrhagic stroke. He has post-traumatic amnesia and right-sided weakness. He does not take any regular medications. Prior to the accident, he worked as an electrician and played amateur football.

On examination, he appears alert but distractible. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He requires assistance for transfers and personal care. He demonstrates insight into his injuries and expresses motivation to return to work, though appears frustrated by his current limitations. His family are supportive and regularly visit.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the most appropriate approach to goal setting in rehabilitation?
options: Goals should be set by the rehabilitation team to ensure they are realistic
Goals should remain fixed throughout rehabilitation for consistency
Goals should be collaboratively set and regularly reviewed
Goals should primarily focus only on physical function
Goals should target a return to pre-injury function
attributes.explanation:
This case highlights the importance of appropriate goal setting in rehabilitation following traumatic brain injury. The patient is young, has supportive family, shows insight and motivation - all positive prognostic factors that should be leveraged in the rehabilitation process.

Goals should be collaboratively set and regularly reviewed represents best practice in rehabilitation medicine. This approach ensures goals are meaningful to the patient while remaining realistic, incorporating both the patient's aspirations (like returning to work) and the clinical team's expertise. Regular review allows goals to be adjusted based on progress and changing circumstances.

Goals should be set by the rehabilitation team without patient input undermines patient autonomy and engagement. While the team provides expertise regarding prognosis and realistic expectations, excluding the patient from goal setting can reduce motivation and compliance with rehabilitation programs.

Goals should remain fixed throughout rehabilitation fails to account for the variable nature of recovery after brain injury. Goals need to be dynamic, adjusting to reflect both progress and setbacks. This patient's goals might evolve from basic self-care to more complex work-related tasks as he improves.

Goals should primarily focus only on physical function is too narrow in scope, particularly for traumatic brain injury. This patient requires goals addressing cognitive function (post-traumatic amnesia), emotional well-being (frustration), and social reintegration (work return) alongside physical rehabilitation.

Goals should target a return to pre-injury function may be unrealistic given the severity of his injuries. While maintaining hope is important, goals must be achievable to prevent disappointment and maintain motivation. A graded approach with progressive goals is more appropriate.

The SMART framework (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) is useful for structuring rehabilitation goals. For this patient, initial goals might focus on independence in self-care, progressing to more complex activities as he improves, always maintaining the longer-term vision of returning to work while remaining realistic about the challenges of severe brain injury.
learningPoints: Goal setting in rehabilitation improves patient motivation, engagement and outcomes through providing direction and measurable targets.
Goals should be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) and collaboratively set with the patient and family.
Regular goal review and adjustment maintains motivation and ensures goals remain appropriate as recovery progresses.
references: Knutti K, Björklund Carlstedt A, Clasen R, Green D. Impacts of goal setting on engagement and rehabilitation outcomes following acquired brain injury: a systematic review of reviews. Disabil Rehabil. 2022;44(12):2581-2590. doi:10.1080/09638288.2020.1846796. Available online at ; https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09638288.2020.1846796#abstract

Doig E, Teo A, Pick V, Libera M, New A, Turner B, Suleman S, Foster M, Hoyle M. Exploring the goals and outcomes of adults with severe acquired brain injury participating in an extended inpatient brain injury rehabilitation unit in Australia. Disability and Rehabilitation. 2024 Jul 10:1-9. Available online at ; https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09638288.2024.2375058#abstract
